title: A six-figure balance, shoulders down
subtitle: A short visualisation for practising the felt sense of a large balance being ordinary.
excerpt: A five-minute visualisation for when a high bank balance still triggers tension rather than ease. The scene: checking the account, seeing the number, and staying calm.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Visualisation as a self-development tool draws on public-domain creative visualisation traditions.

content.text: A five-minute visualisation from the Day 36 MONEY theme. The scene is deliberate: you are looking at a large balance and nothing is happening. Nothing needs to be done. The number just sits there.
Sit or lie down. Close your eyes. One breath in, one out.
Picture yourself at your usual spot for checking accounts: a kitchen table, a desk, a sofa. Morning. Cup of tea or coffee beside you. Phone or laptop in hand.
You open the banking app. The balance is high. Six figures. Let it be specific: pick a number that represents more than you currently hold, but not so large it feels fictional. Your own figure.

Notice what happens in your body when you see it. Let the scene hold for a moment. Then, in the visualisation, let the shoulders drop. Not because you've forced them down, because the number being there is simply ordinary. It was there yesterday. It will be there tomorrow. Nothing is coming for it.
You close the app. You take a sip. The morning continues.
Hold the scene for two minutes. Then open your eyes.
Original to homemade.education. Creative visualisation is an old self-help tradition. This practice puts it to work on the specific feeling of holding a large sum with ease.
